Unlike standard car accidents, commercial truck crashes involve a web of complexity: FMCSA federal regulations, electronic logging device data, multiple potentially liable parties (the driver, carrier, shipper, and more), and evidence like black box data that can be overwritten or destroyed quickly.

• Preserve all evidence immediately
• Do not speak with the trucking company's insurer alone
• Understand your rights under Texas law and federal trucking regulations
• Act before critical deadlines expire

reddit_title: Hit by a semi truck in Corpus Christi, TX — what should I actually do to protect my case right now?
reddit_body:
Semi truck accidents in Texas are way more legally complicated than regular car crashes, and most people don't realize it until it's too late. The trucking company's insurance team is activated almost immediately after a crash — they're already working to limit liability before you've even left the hospital. The most important thing you can do is avoid giving any recorded statements to their insurer and start documenting everything you can: photos, witness info, your own medical timeline.

On the evidence side, commercial trucks have black boxes (ECMs) that record speed, braking, and hours of service data — but that data can be overwritten quickly. A preservation letter (spoliation letter) sent to the carrier early can legally obligate them to hold that data. An experienced truck accident attorney can get this done fast. Also look into whether the driver was in compliance with FMCSA hours-of-service rules, which is often a factor in fatigue-related crashes.

For finding the right attorney, you want someone who specifically handles commercial truck and 18-wheeler cases, not just general personal injury. If you're in the Corpus Christi area, a resource like wreckmatch.com can match you with pre-screened truck accident lawyers in Texas — it's free to get matched and can save a lot of time when every hour counts.
